items.q: Which preposition expresses purpose ('in order to')?
items.why: Students treat por and para as identical 'for'. PARA = destination, purpose, recipient, deadline. POR = reason/cause, exchange, duration, motion through. The distinction is regularly tested.
Another classic faux ami. Spanish 'constipado' refers to nasal congestion (a cold). The medical 'constipated' is 'estreñido'. Confusion can lead to awkward medical conversations.

items.q: What is the difference between 'tu' and 'tú'?
items.misconception: They are identical with optional accent.
items.random_wrong: Tú is informal, tu is formal.
items.why: Students think accents are decorative. The accent distinguishes meaning: tu hermano (your brother) vs tú eres (you are). Similar pairs: si (if) vs sí (yes); el (the) vs él (he); mi (my) vs mí (me).

items.q: Which figure shows a gap between statement and meaning?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Ironia. “Simbolo” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What does the moon often symbolise in Lorca?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Death. “Love” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many figures of speech are introduced in this stamp?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
